name: The Milverton Archive
description: The extensive collection of compromising letters, indiscreet communications, and damaging personal documents amassed by Charles Augustus Milverton in his long career as a blackmailer. The archive represents years of systematic acquisition — Milverton employed agents, cultivated servants, and paid informants to obtain letters that their authors would pay almost any price to suppress. Holmes is hired to recover a letter from the archive on behalf of a client; after burgling Milverton's Hampstead home (and witnessing his murder at the hands of a woman he had previously ruined), Holmes burns the entire collection in the fireplace. He makes no attempt to return individual documents to their authors.
significance: Holmes's decision to burn the entire archive — rather than selectively returning documents — is a characteristically extreme act of private justice. He tells Watson afterward that he refuses to assist the police in identifying Milverton's murderer, regarding her as having performed a public service. The Milverton case is one of the sharpest illustrations of Holmes's willingness to operate entirely outside the law when he judges the moral case sufficient.
